english: 1. also rtá-mtsa-lu (rta mtsha lu) Lex., Sch.: a horse with white feet. -- 2. v. tsa-lu (tsha lu).
Sch. = tsags (tshags) 1, tsags-bzáṅ (tshags bzang) byéd-pa (byed pa).

1. resp. for miṅ (ming), name, esp. the new name which every one receives that takes orders; mtsan (mtshan) gsól-ba (gsol ba) 1. to give a name Glr. 2. to take, to assume, a name Glr., title W. -- 2. mark, sign, v. mtsan-ma (mtshan ma). -- 3. night, mtsán-mo (mtshan mo).

1. sign (rtags (rtags) and ltas (ltas)), mark, token, badge, symptom, dón-med-pai (don med pa'i) mtsán-ma (mtshan ma) yin (yin) it is a sign that it would be fruitless Wdṅ.; mtsán-ma (mtshan ma) ₒdébs-pa ('debs pa) to make a mark, to mark (e.g. with paint) Glr.; btsún-mo-la (btsun mo la) ma (ma) ₒjigs-śig ('jigs shig) byás-pai (byas pa'i) mtsán-ma (mtshan ma) byín-nas (byin nas) making a sign to the queen, signifying: do not fear! (that she had nothing to fear); mtsán-mas (mtshan mas) mtsón-pa (mtshon pa) to represent a thing by a sign or mark Lex.; rgyal-poi (rgyal po'i) mtsán-ma (mtshan ma) (or rtags (rtags)) lṅá-po (lnga po) (acc. to Indian notions) the five royal insignia, turban, parasol, sword, fly-flap and coloured sandals; shape and peculiar characteristics of separate parts of the body, lus-kyi (lus kyi) mtsan (mtshan) Dzl. 724, 5, esp. as marks of beauty, skyés-bu (skyes bu) čén-poi (chen po'i) mtsan (mtshan) sum-ču-rtsa-gñis (sum cu rtsa gnyis) cf. skyés-bu (skyes bu); mtsan (mtshan) daṅ (dang) dbyibs (dbyibs) as to limbs and stature Dzl.; mtsan(-ma) (mtshan (-ma)) bzáṅ(-po) (bzang (-po)) and ṅán(-pa) (ngan (-pa)) good and evil signs, tokens, symptoms, prognostics, frq.; bkra-śis-pai (bkra shis pa'i) dge-mtsan (dge mtshan) propitious signs Glr., emphat., good, favourable sign, some special (good) quality, mtsan (mtshan) daṅ (dang) ldan-pa (ldan pa) possessing such quality, superior, excellent, frq.; mtsán-ma (mtshan ma) rtóg-pa (rtog pa) to prove, to examine, signs; mtsán-mar (mtshan mar) sgóm-pa (sgom pa) to take as an omen Sch., mtsán-mar (mtshan mar) ma (ma) bzuṅ (bzung) do not regard it as an (evil) omen, be not surprised or alarmed Sch. - mtsan(daṅ) (mtshan (dang)) bcás(-pa) (bcas (-pa)), and mtsan-méd (mtshan med) having characteristics and having none, (v. also Was. 297), terms with which Buddhist speculation loves to play, cf. Köpp. I, 597. -- 2. genitals Med., Pth., gen. preceded by p̉o (pho) or mo (mo); mtsan-dbye (mtshan dbye) prob. the genitals open themselves Med.; hence in Lhasa the word tsan-zúg (tshan zug) (q.v.) might be misunderstood for painful affection of the genitals. -- 3. śin-tu (shin tu) mtsan (mtshan) čé-bar (che bar) gyúr-te (gyur te) is at one time applied to Buddha, at another to men, thus leaving the true meaning doubtful. Comp. and deriv. mtsán-mk̉an (mtshan mkhan) soothsayer, astrologer, frq. - mtsan-gráṅ (mtshan grang) and dgu-mtsan (dgu mtshan) prize, crown of victory C. - mtsan-brjód (mtshan brjod) calling upon the name of a deity, enumerating its characteristics and attributes Cs.; mtsan-dón (mtshan don) something similar(?). - mtsan-ñyid (mtshan nyid) prop.: 'the sign', the essential characteristic, sometimes even implying the true, innermost essence of a thing, whilst, on the other hand, it is also used merely for 'mark' in general; čós-kyi (chos kyi) mtsan-ñyid (mtshan nyid) stón-pa (ston pa), kỏṅ-du (khong du) čúd-pa (chud pa) prob. to show the true essence of doctrine, to receive it into one's own mind Dzl.; mtsan-ñyid-pa (mtshan nyid pa) Mil. n. of a philosophical school of the present day, stated to be the same as bye-brág-pa (bye brag pa); it is much in favour with the Gelugpa-sect, and the principal object of their studies is, to ascertain the literal sense and original spirit of their doctrine; they love disputations on these subjects, and may be considered the representatives of speculative science among the Tibetan clergy. - ₒdus-byás-kyi ('dus byas kyi) mtsan-ñyid (mtshan nyid) mi-rtág-pa (mi rtag pa) yin (yin) the essential property of all that is compounded is liability to decay Glr.; property, quality Domaṅ (mdo mang); symptom, indication, nád-pa (nad pa) sós-pai (sos pa'i) mtsan-ñyid (mtshan nyid) an indication that the patient will recover S.g.; mtsan-ñyid (mtshan nyid) gsum (gsum) the three marks or characteristics in the doctrine of 'perception' of the Mahayanists, kun-btágs (kun btags), gźán-dbáṅ (gzhan dbang), yoṅs-grúb (yongs grub) Was. 291; mtsan-ñyid (mtshan nyid) bśád-pa (bshad pa) Schr.: definition; so it seems to be used in Thgy. - mtsan-rtágs (mtshan rtags) = mtsán-ma (mtshan ma) Wdk. - mtsán-pa (mtshan pa) marked, ₒkór-los ('khor los) being marked with the figure of a wheel Glr. - mtsán-dpe (mtshan dpe) for mtsan (mtshan) daṅ (dang) dpe-byad (dpe byad) Glr. - mtsan-gźi (mtshan gzhi) Lex., Sch.: 'the cause of a sign or symptom, an object' (?).
W., *tsan* (mtshan), night *tsan* (mtshan) *ča* (cha) *dug* (dug), W. night sets in; adv. at
